name: ASCII Chart
description: ASCII and braille character-based charts for terminal-style data visualization.

line.description: ASCII line chart with labeled axes, dot-and-dash data points, and optional series legend

bar.description: Horizontal ASCII bar chart using block fill characters with value labels and color legend

visual_brief: Monospace terminal-style chart rendered entirely with ASCII and braille characters on a dark or transparent background. Green-on-dark color scheme by default. Line preset draws axes with pipe and dash characters, data points with dots and asterisks connected by line-drawing characters. Bar preset renders horizontal bars using block characters (full, three-quarter, half, quarter fills). Sparkline preset is a single inline row of braille dots. All presets include a chart title at top and axis labels along edges.

responsive.mobile: Chart width scales to container. Character count per row reduces. Sparkline preset preferred for inline metrics. Legend moves below chart.
responsive.tablet: Standard chart dimensions. Side legend if space allows.
responsive.desktop: Full-width charts with generous character grids. Legend renders beside the chart area.

layout_hints.chart_role: This pattern is a terminal-native data summary. Keep labels, legend, and plot tightly coupled so the user reads it as one instrument rather than as styled text blocks.
layout_hints.character_density: Preserve legibility of the character grid before adding extra annotations. On smaller widths, reduce series density rather than cramming more glyphs.
layout_hints.sparkline_use: Sparkline mode should act as supportive inline telemetry, not as the primary analytical surface.

motion.micro: Realtime updates may redraw or pulse subtly to indicate fresh data, but the chart should remain readable and stable.
motion.transitions: Data changes should feel like continuous terminal output rather than animated infographic motion.

keyboard: If the chart is interactive, keyboard focus should move to the chart summary or legend rather than each individual glyph.
announcements: Expose chart title, series names, and key value summaries semantically so the visualization is not dependent on character art alone.
